hyperliquid_hype_bullish.summary: HYPE token surged from ~$45 to $72. Flipped DOGE to enter top 10 by market cap. Spot HYPE trading on Solana hit $56M, ranking it above Coinbase and OKX. Multiple CT voices bullish; Udi Wertheimer skeptical of excessive hype but acknowledges product quality. Jake Chervinsky launched @HyperliquidPC lobbying org. Simon Dedic and others shilling CARDS as 'the Hyperliquid of small caps'.

bitcoin.summary: Bitcoin at $73K per Laura Shin/Bits+Bips. Crypto_McKenna flagging quantum computing threat to Bitcoin as underappreciated institutional risk. Saylor criticized for silence on quantum migration. Conviction-based vs ETF-tourist narrative prominent.

ethereum_bearish_rotation.summary: Crypto_McKenna making a strong bear case: rotation out of ETH began ~6 months ago, sees path to $1,000. DAT companies will sell ETH on their balance sheets with no buy-side demand to absorb. Evan_ss6 also casually bearish (BTC 40k, ETH 800 scenario floated).

solana.summary: Beaniemaxi posted that Solana is 'basically cooked' due to DeFi hacks and memecoin rugs. Counter: HYPE spot volume on Solana surging. Udi Wertheimer suggested Hyperliquid add SOL deposit natively.

tcg_cards_gacha_on_chain.summary: Significant cluster: $CARDS (Collector Crypt) and $GACHA (powergacha_sol) going parabolic. Bluntz up 265% on $CARDS. $GACHA described as a 'hip-3 moment' for TCGs — 100% of trading fees buy graded Pokemon packs, random winner gets slabs every 30 min. ColdBloodShill, zeroxkyle, Zeneca, sjdedic all involved. loomdart writing extended thesis that Pokemon cards represent a broader societal shift in consumer spending toward collectibles/investment goods.

zcash_zec_technical.summary: Bluntz_Capital calling $ZEC all-time highs by end of next week based on Elliott Wave: clear ABC correction, 5-subwave C, 4h bull divergence, bounce off 4h 200MA.

defi_small_caps.summary: thedefivillain covering $LAB (alleged $9B FDV scam being pumped) and $STG squeezing shorts post-ZRO migration. Udi Wertheimer noting ordinals always pull people back.

oil_energy_iran_crisis.summary: biancoresearch (Jim Bianco) writing extensively: Strait of Hormuz closure being treated as 60-day temporary problem but is potentially structural. Governments draining inventories to bridge gap — 'burning lifeboats to build a temporary bridge.' Cites parallel to every major crisis where Wall Street treated structural problems as liquidity glitches. pboockvar amplifying commodity scarcity thesis. zriboua noting IRGC using time-buying tactics but facing unprecedented combined military + economic pressure.

equities_summer_meltup.summary: stevehou thesis: 'summer meltup' started earlier in 2026 because exogenous shock (Liberation Day Apr 2 + Iran conflict Feb 28) happened earlier than prior year's equivalent. 9 straight weeks of record highs. SpotGamma: mid-June triple threat from SpaceX IPO + FOMC + major OPEX. krugermacro: 'it's called booming not peaking.'

ai_economy_labor.summary: hooeem debunking a Wharton/BU paper 'The AI Layoff Trap' which claims AI will mathematically destroy the economy — argues the model assumes money saved from job displacement vanishes and requires knife-edge conditions. RaoulGMI: AI race cannot be stopped by any government, game theory means US and China must both accelerate.

models_and_labs.summary: EMostaque reviewed Claude Opus 4.8: says 'worry less about being turned into paper clips, more about being annoyed to death' but likes Claude 4.6 Max. PeterDiamandis: Opus 4.8 scored 57.9% on Humanity's Last Exam — their own AGI threshold was 50%. cryptopunk7213: OpenAI released Rosalind (weapons-grade biodefense model) selectively to government partners; being used by CEPI to develop Ebola vaccine in <100 days. nicbstme: ran OpenAI Codex for 11+ hours on Navier-Stokes Millennium Prize problem — describes it as 'psychological warfare.'

forward_deployed_engineers.summary: cryptopunk7213: Forward Deployed Engineer (FDE) is hottest AI job — $300K base before equity, up 15-20% YoY. Palantir biggest hirer; Cursor, Softbank, Notion listed 4,000 new FDE roles this year. Anthropic and OpenAI plan to hire thousands.

ai_infrastructure_photonics_semis.summary: aleabitoreddit: NVDA GTC/Computex in Taipei expected to reveal next AI bottleneck in optical/photonics. $AAOI (Applied Optoelectronics) at $150 — personal conviction for 4-5x in 12-24M based on laser fab bottlenecks, GS optical TAM projections, Made in America push, ATM overhang as main headwind. $ARM at $354 (from $134 entry); NVDA unveiling ARM-based processors at Computex; GPU:CPU ratios requiring more CPUs. Nippon Chemical (4092/red phosphorus for InP substrates) up ~100% — called as choke-point-of-choke-point for hyperscaler buildout. dylan522p posting about NVIDIA NX1.

colombia_election.summary: Geiger_Capital extensively covering Colombian presidential election: far-right Abelardo de la Espriella won first round outright (~44%) — no poll predicted it. Paloma Valencia (7%) and former president Uribe endorsed Abelardo. Now heavy favorite in June 21 runoff vs Ivan Cepeda (41%). Geiger subscribers were apparently positioned months in advance.

china_iran_military.summary: zriboua sharing/commenting on report: Chinese-made MANPAD shoulder-launched missile likely hit the F-15E Strike Eagle shot down over southwest Iran in April. China also supplied Iran with long-range early-warning radar capable of detecting stealth aircraft. Significant escalation in US-China relations context.

biotech_rvmd_cancer.summary: Citrini: $RVMD (Revolution Medicines) presenting at ASCO26 — Phase 3 RASolute 302 trial shows daraxonrasib significantly extends OS and PFS vs chemo in previously-treated metastatic pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma. Called as a 2026 trade. 'Copy-paste' potential of RAS-ON inhibition beyond pancreatic cancer is key re-rating catalyst.

homebuilders_berkshire.summary: Jim Cramer: Berkshire Hathaway buying Taylor Morrison (homebuilder) — calling it a signal of value in the 'hated homebuilder' sector. Floats idea of Berkshire deploying Clayton prefab homes on Taylor Morrison land.

tesla_fsd.summary: stevehou: Tesla FSD 'Mad Max' mode — drives to front of exit ramp queue, squeezes into ramp ahead of others. Commentary that Tesla wins on self-driving because Elon willing to push boundaries Waymo won't.

spacex_ipo_volatility.summary: SpotGamma flagging SpaceX IPO as one of three mid-June volatility catalysts alongside FOMC and major OPEX. SmallCapScience: $SPCE (Virgin Galactic) up 20% in overnight markets.

software_strategy_mstr_strc.summary: Quinn Thompson: $STRC (Strategy preferred) vs $MSTR dynamic — for June, Strategy prioritizing MSTR owners over STRC owners. MSTR has been down since STRC launch. stevehou retweeting Bloomberg earnings inflection index bullish on software names.

allin_liquidity_summit.summary: The All-In Podcast running 'All-In Liquidity' summit. Gifting suite sponsors: Tecovas, Tru Niagen, Omnilux LED, HigherDose, Velocity Black, Caldera+Lab. Sold out quickly; early bird pricing now available for 2027.

notable_narratives.description: Multiple credible CT voices confirming HYPE entered top 10 by market cap, flipping DOGE. Spot trading on Solana surging to $56M/24h, above Coinbase and OKX. Jake Chervinsky launched formal lobbying org (HyperliquidPC). Broad consensus that tradfi is now buying what CT sold.

$CARDS and $GACHA going parabolic. loomdart writing extended investment thesis that Pokemon cards represent a broader consumer shift from aspirational luxury spending to collectibles-as-investment. Multiple high-conviction CT traders in the trade.

Jim Bianco warning that treating Hormuz closure as temporary liquidity problem while draining inventories is dangerous. Could mirror every past crisis where structural issues were misidentified as transient. pboockvar and commodity-focused accounts amplifying.
Crypto_McKenna making structured bear case for ETH toward $1,000 — DAT company treasury unwind + no institutional demand to absorb sell flow. Shift from 6 months ago when he resisted this view.

notable_narratives.narrative: AGI threshold crossed per Peter Diamandis
notable_narratives.description: Diamandis stated on MOONSHOTS podcast that 50% on Humanity's Last Exam = AGI. Claude Opus 4.8 scored 57.9%. Anthropic crossed their self-defined threshold. EMostaque more skeptical/sardonic in review.

notable_narratives.narrative: China arms Iran with MANPAD + stealth radar
notable_narratives.description: Chinese-made MANPAD likely downed US F-15E over Iran. China also supplied long-range stealth-detection radar to Iran. Significant escalation in US-China proxy conflict context.

Crypto_McKenna: quantum threat to BTC is a blocker for institutional flows and changes underwriting risk. Criticizes Saylor for not leading on migration plan. Post-CLARITY (crypto legislation) alignment on migration path could bring significant inflows.

stevehou: AI-turbocharged US economy is an 'inflated basketball' that was temporarily submerged by exogenous shocks (Liberation Day, Iran war). Once shocks resolve, melt-up continues. SpotGamma flags triple threat in mid-June: SpaceX IPO, FOMC, major OPEX as vol catalysts.

OpenAI releasing Rosalind (previously deemed too dangerous for public release) to select government/health partners. Currently being used by CEPI to develop Ebola vaccine in under 100 days. First use of private AI lab in national health defense.

ZachXBT: major Meta AI support vulnerability over the weekend allowed password reset on any IG account without 2FA and without identity verification. Now patched. Telegram black-market IG services monetized heavily from it.

I think we’ll hear about the next AI bottleneck.

Is now required for optical alignment requirements for CPO.

I went long last year with low sizing at $28, back when I guessed they were qualifying with $AMZN and $MSFT.

High conviction post earnings at ~$70, when they announced 1.6T and other volume orders with hyperscalers.

Capacity projections at $90 for 2027 timelines were bullish. 

Now at $150, the story from 2025 is coming together with all the laser fab bottlenecks, GS optical TAM projections, Made in America efforts, $NVDA / $AMD discussion rumors. 

The only thing holding them back is ATM after ATM, and now another $600m ATM… 

I personally think it easily rerates once the mechanical selling pressure stops.

I think we’ve begun the rotation out of Ethereum. I think this started around 6 months ago.

I’ve seen a large amount of bear takes on Ethereum calling for 3 digits and felt resistance to it. But now I do genuinely believe we are just starting to see the rotation out of Ethereum.

I’m not sure how long it takes but I do see Ethereum going towards $1000. Every under water DAT will sell ETH on their balance and buyback their own shares. No one is buying these sell flows.

If anything people are going to rotate early and it’s going to unravel slowly and there is no demand to meet it.

Bitcoin has to solve its quantum threat and provide a viable path for what to do with effected public keys that can be used to obtain a wallets private key.

This is a major blocker for flows and changes underwriting risk for how much BTC Institutions are willing to hold.

Equally post CLARITY if the core contributors can align on a migration plan I believe this would bring sizable inflows.

I remain puzzled on why Saylor one of the largest public holders of Bitcoin has not been proactive around this conversation.

It’s not a non-issue and head in the sand for longer increases risks and reduces flows.

all_tweets.text: @0xMentalIllness @d_gilz Same hah

Here is my take, which is consistent with the Goehring & Rozencwajg letter in the repost?
----
Every crisis—1997, 2008, 2020—starts with the same institutional script: treating a structural problem as a temporary liquidity glitch. Wall Street is hardwired to assume that everything will safely revert to a mean, fearing the phrase "this time is different." It takes months of frustration to realize the rules of the game have changed.

Today, I fear we are repeating this exact behavioral trap. 

Believing the closure of the Strait of Hormuz is a brief, 60-day hurdle, governments and oil companies are aggressively draining inventories to bridge the gap. They are treating a potential permanent, structural deficit in the world’s crude supply as a short-term liquidity problem.  

Burning the lifeboats to build a temporary bridge only works if a resolution is guaranteed. By artificially delaying gradual demand destruction today, we ensure it hits all at once tomorrow should inventories slam into operational minimums.  

Why is the market so blind to this? Because over the last five years, macro investors have developed deep scar tissue from listening to "experts":

* Q2 2020: Virologists scared investors into selling at the pandemic market bottom.

* April 2020: Commodity bears screamed that oil was irrelevant right as it hit its historic generational low.  

* H2 2022: Economists panicked investors into dumping equities at the peak of 9% inflation.

* Q2 2023: Banking analysts screamed sell at the market bottom following Silicon Valley Bank's failure.

* April 2025, Policy Analysts scared investors about tariffs and Liberation Day to sell the low of the year.

The market learned that when the experts scream trouble, buying the dip makes money, and hedging for disaster loses it. 

But ignoring the energy sector today, investors might be confusing past false alarms with a looming physical reality ... tanks really are running dry, and prospects for the Strait to "normalize" are nonexistent.

Within 60 days, if not less than 30 days. 

Here is one "why."
--
Last week on Bloomberg TV, Chevron CEO Mike Wirth warned that despite ongoing peace talks, persistent shipping disruptions and kinetic risks in the Strait of Hormuz remain very real. 

He emphasized that the global system is structurally tight, citing U.S. distillate and gasoline inventories at their lowest levels since 2003, with zero spare refining capacity to replenish them. 

Crucially, he noted that even if a diplomatic resolution is reached, independent shipowners and cautious insurers will be deeply hesitant to rush back in, preventing any immediate return to normal supply chains.

all_tweets.text: @sjdedic missed a perfect opportunity to explain the hip-3 moment
slide decks = adult picture books
@BlaznovX it’s called speculation my g

this is the kind of content this X algorithm likes to reward: fear mongering.

because that’s all it is when the paper argues AI will wreck the economy because firms fire workers, and workers who lose income stop buying things.

the maths quietly assumes the money saved from job displacement just vanishes. owners never spend it, no new jobs appear, and nothing ever gets cheaper or better.

that saved money doesn’t disappear: it gets reinvested, paid out, or shows up as lower prices, which creates new demand and new jobs elsewhere.

it also assumes people spend half their income on one single industry’s products, which is wildly unrealistic.

the paper even admits its scariest result needs special knife-edge conditions, and that letting money recirculate makes the problem shrink toward nothing.

if you read this post you’d feel slightly anxious, if you read the article or even put it into a frontier model and asked it to break it down then you’d wonder what the

Pokémon cards are the ice breaker in a far larger societal trend change 

Why spend money on something that loses value when you can spend it on something that gains value 

This trends gotten more and more apparent with brands like supreme etc taking off but feels like it never

all_tweets.text:
Pokémon cards are the ice breaker in a far larger societal trend change 

Why spend money on something that loses value when you can spend it on something that gains value 

This trends gotten more and more apparent with brands like supreme etc taking off but feels like it never really broke out into the finance side

This has changed now with pokemon cards truly breaking out, the next few years the entire world of commerce is gonna get drastically shaken up

people tend to spend more money on investments vs consumption

the tricky part is unless you were into jewelery or watches, there wasn't really anything to buy like so

Sure you could be a person who spent alot of money on designer clothes or sneakers, but these products kind of messed up their collectability factor by constantly accomodating to new levels of demand (basically making stuff to ensure their collectors continously got dumped on and scalped thru etc shareholder benefit but collectors loss)

there hasn't really been one solid collectible that wasn't cartel operated (diamonds) a artificial scarcity scam (rolex, ap etc) or aggressively niche/stylised (can argue supreme only hits that hypebeast crowd, too thematic/topical)

Pokemon cards unironically the first collectible that has no real fuggazzi intrinsic value premise, its just pure collectible (which it fell into by somewhat of a coincidence, the fact they're game cards and focused on being game cards was a big part)

add in the fact that consumers have just gotten way smarter (treat nba players as an example, 20-30 years ago it was giant diamonds and chains, now its investments and collectibles) and think generally entire consumer base is becoming far more open and excited for this new age of consumer goods/collectibles

the way i've been thinking about it is alot of consumer spending was aspirational purchasing of luxury goods to flex

this glass house has however collapsed. us tariffs  causing chinese manufacturers to go on war path and talk about how they can make luxury goods for fractions of price etc did legitimate harm to the concept of luxury goods. Quality is no longer an excuse to charge a super high water mark, people no longer feel cool going to the lengths they would go to to buy these luxury items 

now we have a big class of wealthy consumers looking for something new to buy, and feels like collectibles is a really good place for that (im seeing this as 2021 for sneakers but done without having a supplier like nike/adidas who are hellbent on making sure they ship as much product as they can, just utterly gutting and suffocating collectors)

pokemon may seem niche, but id say its unniche enough to break thru the ice which its doing

also this whole idea of "its valuable because its valuable, not its valuable because its rare or controlled by a cartel or has a cost to produce" is becoming more and more apparent 

the old guard are dying, diamonds r collapsing, and in that a very very hungry consumer base will look for something else to satiate their needs 

pokemon cards really just the start in this imo, it'll keep expanding (and this is something that crypto is incredibly uniquely suited to dominate, it just needs to be done properly)

have some fun stuff to announce next week, then we get this ball rolling

it is going to be a truly tremendous summer

I miss these good old days. You sit in a warehouse with hundreds of students for hours with just a pen and paper. 

You need intense concentration and stamina. You can go to the bathroom, but you better run to not waste time. Every minute matters. 

The exams are over 3 days. It’s psychological warfare. I remembered looking at all the people and panicking because I knew there were only 17 spots open. 

I don’t know if I could do it again. But I love the subject and I can imagine the staff saying out loud « the authority of science, you have 6 hours »

Because I have a 15 months old kid and I love to walk/bike everywhere (I’m from France) my favorites are Duboce Triangle, Lower Haight, the Panhandle, and Cole Valley because it’s walkable, quiet, beautiful, and conveniently close to everything: grocery stores, restaurants, parks. 

If you don’t care about walkability/parks then Pacific Heights or Russian Hill are awesome. Fillmore too. 

I find Dogpatch a bit soulless but it’s clean and nice and next to OAI office. 

TL;DR: it depends on your lifestyle. If you like to bike/surf everyday or prefer the nightlife action then it’s different neighborhood.
